author: @craigywaigy4703
text:
Why have you moulded a SOLID trefoil - the principle behind all of these mechanisms is well known, and as you alluded to, they function like a gear train, by the virtue of static friction between the rollers at the interface between rollers and banding.
You can do exactly the same thing with chain(duplex) and single staggered row sprockets to create very complex systems using simple means. 
A classic example is the planetary reduction box, where the external ring gear can actually be a chain welted onto a carrier frame - this is ideal where access to advanced machinetools, or expensive equipment is not possible.
 Nice explanation, but the rigid trefoil "band" really cocks up the conveyancing of the principles(and it doesn't work eirher)? 
🤔👍

Possibly useful :: 
There was a long term test with a wire based Nitinol motor, one thing surprising from the study was the durability of the wire, which was expected to fail after some number of revolutions of the system.
Surprisingly it did not appear to deteriorate over time and it was noted that it improved in performance as the test went on until the experiment was ended.

author: @scottmiller2591
text: I've been fascinated with the Rolamite ever since I saw it in NASA tech briefs (as I recall).  It is a beautiful mechanism, and has _extremely_ low rolling loss as long as a nice band of thin tempered spring metal or the like is used.  Unfortunately, although I've played around with it for decades, I never really came up with a practical use for the thing.  I think a couple or more of them would work nicely as a long throw linear motor bearing, but I've always found that for the small throws of motors I was designing, flexures worked better (I've got a few patents for such motors used in implantable hearing aid stimulators that are in use commercially).  The big problem with the rotary form is you need a continuous band.  The obvious way to make a band from a strip is to spot weld it, and that leads to all kinds of issues - a bump in thickness, poor spring tempering of the weld, etc.  I supposed at one point a good way to make the belt was from Kapton tape laid down on a mandrel with a inner release layer of Kapton that had no adhesive, which lets it be thin, strong, good energy recover, durable, and inextensible.  Making it out of TPU as you have gets around the issue of bonding the ends of a strip, although then you have to deal with the abysmal properties of the plastic (set, cold flowing, terrible energy recovery, thickness, and durability) - pretty much the opposite of Kapton) in this application.  I look forward to your video inspiring some tinkerers out there to figure out some interesting solutions and applications.  Good hunting to all you tinkerers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thank you for taking the time to post and i agree it is a fascinating mechanism - TPU is great for a demo but as you say pretty poor for a practical device - what do you think of one of the carbon fibre filled plastics as an idea?

replies.author: @scottmiller2591
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Well, carbon filled would be better for inextensibility, true.  I'm not a materials expert, but can you wrap carbon filled materials around that radius without having it break down/wearing out?  It sounds like a try-it-and-see situation.

author: @icebluscorpion
text: 6:30 you are not that quite right there flexing material will hold forever if you stay in the elastic deformation region at all circumstances. You can bend rigid titanium pipes infinitely es long as the material is monolith and monocrysatline and you stay at all cost in the elastic region of the specific material. This is method science is called Complient mechanism. The key of eternal flexible mechanisms is to stay in the elastic region of a monolith, monocrysatline material!

replies.text: cheers mate for adding that correction

author: @spokehedz
text: These mechanisms were used in early floppy drives to turn a rotation motion (from the stepper motors) into a linear motion to move the heads from track to track. It was found that the band/belt would snap, and then nothing could be done to fix the unit without sending it back. So the units that used a modified lead-screw design ultimately took over.

author: @guytech7310
text: Robert: How about this: remove the ribbon & replace with carriers on the roller sides (like you did for the outer Red carrier. Then make the carriers locked or rotatable. Then I think your got a compact variable speed drive. Lock the inner blue roller carrier to increase speed Now it would interesting if you could use some sort of gear linkage between the two carriers to create even more speed differences, perhaps even continuous variable speed adjustments.

author: @1kreature
text:
That was so close! Reading the original paper you see the important bit is the rollers must be small enough so that the roller + band does not touch both the upper/lower or inner/outer edge at same time, not just that they must be big enough to not pass eachother. The inner and outer rollers will be pushed towards their respective surfaces by the tension in the band.
When you were rotating your tests it seemed like there was a lot of friction. Looked like this was from the band passing under the outer red rollers touching the inner roller, which would be traveling the opposite way causing friction. I would love to see this again with either slightly smaller red rollers, or bigger diameter outer ring together with a tighter band. See if it really is almost frictionless like it states.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - but I am not sure the roller size would make a difference there - i am not sure but i think it is the band tension that governs that - I didn't add a band tensioner - which would be an awesome addition - of course I used TPU for the example and that is a bit stretchy here but makes a great demo - but perhaps not a great finished item - but you do raise a really interesting point and thanks for pointing that out
